docs.title: Electricity
docs.text:
The electrical nature of matter is inherent in atomic structure.

Proton's charge: = (Coulombs)

Electron's charge: =

Neutron's charge: none

Removing an electron from a neutral atom (where number of protons = number of electrons) yields a positively charged ion, called electrification. This results in a transfer of charge, not a creation of charge.

Charge can neither be created or destroyed.

  • Law of Conservation of Electrical Charge

docs.title: Charged Objects and the Electrical Force
docs.text:
Objects with excess charge will exert forces on each other.

  • Electrical Force (like gravitational force)

Opposite charges attract, like charges repel. There are two types - attractive and repulsive.

Newton's 2nd Law: , Electric charges can accelerate forces.

docs.title: Conductors & Insulators
docs.text:
Conductors and insulators are determined by atomic structure. The valence (outermost) electrons are more weakly bound to the nucleus. They can "break free" and move through the material. These are called conduction electrons.

(negative charged object: ) --> ~conductor~ --> (positively charged obj: )

Electrons will continue to flow until the charge on each object is equal.

, so each object must end up with a charge of .

Generally, good thermal conductors are good electrical conductors.

Conductors - copper, silver, salt water, gold, iron Insulators - rubber, glass, wood, plastic

docs.title: Charging an Object
docs.text:
• Two ways to charge -
    1. Charging by contact: touch a positively charged object with a negatively charged object, or vice versa
    2. Charging by induction: bring a negatively charged object close to a positively charged object, or vice versa.
  • (does not work for insulators, instead forms dipoles) - called Polarization
  • Dipoles: two equal but opposite charges separated by some distance .
  • produces static cling by forming a slight positive charge

docs.title: Coulomb's Law
docs.text:
Coulomb's Law: (watch the magnitude! don't negate) Point charges: charges that are much smaller than the distance separating them.

What if there's 3 charges? -- Then the net force on one charge is the vector sum of the other two forces.

docs.title: Electric Field
docs.text:
A charge creates an electric field that fills all space. Any other charge in that field will feel a force. - Similarly, in a gravitational field, the earth fills all space, and the moon feels the effect of this field. - Stationary charges create electric fields that fill all space. - Other (non-stationary) charges will feel forces in these electric fields.

i.e. the electric field is force per unit charge:

If we place a charge in an electric field, it will feel a force given by . is created by other charges, not q.

docs.title: Electric Field of a Point Charge
docs.text:
Electric field of a point charge:

What is the magnitude of the acceleration of a particle of mass and charge placed between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor of charge density ?

docs.title: Electric Field Lines
docs.text:
Electric fields lines always start at and are directed away from positive charges and always end at and are directed toward negative charges.

  1. Lines start from positive charges and end on negative charges.
  2. The electric field vector at some point in space is always tangent to the field line at that point.
  3. The more lines you have per unit volume (density), the stronger the field.
  4. The number of field lines is proportional to the magnitude of the charge.

docs.title: Parallel Plate Capacitor
docs.text:
Consider a metallic plate with a total charge distributed uniformly over its surface. If the face of the plate has a surface area , then where is the surface charge density.

Now let's take two identical plates, one with total charge and one with total charge . So the magnitude of is for each plate. In between the plates, there's a uniform electric field that points from the positive plate to the negative one. This is a parallel plate capacitor.

Electric field between the plates: .

The field doesn't depend on the distance from the charged plates. It's uniform, but only near the middle of the plates. This is a fringe field.

docs.title: Electric Field Inside a Conductor
docs.text:
Let's place a bunch of electrons at the center of a solid conducting sphere. Repulsion spreads electrons out! So, they move to the surface.

At equilibrium under electrostatic conditions, any excess charge resides on the surface of the conductor.

The interior of the conductor still has electrons, but they are compensated exactly by the positive changes in the interior, so the interior is electrically neutral. Since the charges are static, the force on them is zero, therefore .

This is a static condition - once the surface charges are induced, they don't move.

This electric field has to be perpendicular to the surface of a conductor.

docs.title: Electric Flux and Gauss' Law
docs.text:
Flux: measure of how much field passes perpendicularly through a surface

i.e. [(electric field)(area)] =

We can now use the concept of electric flux and Gauss' Law to determine the electric field. Let's start with a positive point charge: .

To use Gauss' Law, you have to pick a surface. For example, for my point charge , I'll enclose it in a spherical radius . This is my surface. - Gaussian surface: surface to which I will apply Gauss' Law - Ideally, it'll have high symmetry, like a sphere or cylinder, so it's easy to calculate the area of the surfaces.

Gauss' Law: The electric flux passing through a Gaussian surface is equal to the charge enclosed by the surface divided by .

(The summation here is to sum over the different areas you have in your Gaussian surface.)

docs.title: Conservative Force
docs.text:
1. The work done on an object by a conservative force depends only on the object's initial and final position, and not the path taken.
  2. The net work done by a conservative force in moving an object around a closed path is zero.

docs.title: Potential Energy
docs.text:
The electric force, like gravity, is a conservative force.

Let's place a positive point charge in a uniform electric field and let it move from the positive plate to the negative plate. How much work is done by the field in moving the charge?

*Remember, , where is the component of the constant force along the direction of the motion. Here, , so .

The work done = change in electrostatic potential energy!

The quantity on the right is the potential energy per unit charge. We call this the electric potential, . - units -

docs.title: Work
docs.text:
1. Work is not a vector, but it can be either positive or negative:
    • Positive - Force is in the same direction as the motion.
    • Negative - Force is in the opposite direction as the motion.
  2. If positive work is done on an object, the object speeds up.
  3. If negative work is done on an object, the object slows down.

docs.title: Electric Potential Difference
docs.text:
We can determine the value of the potential at different points in space. For example, what is the difference in electrostatic potential between two points, A and B, in an electric field? electric potential , so .

Let's say the charge at point A is positive. If I release it, it'll move down towards B. Since the force is down and the motion is down, positive work is done on the charge, so is positive. This means is negative, or . Point A is at a higher potential than point B.

  • Positive charges, starting from rest, will move away from high potential regions and move toward low potential regions.
  • Negative charges, starting from rest, will move away from low potential regions and move toward high potential regions.

docs.title: Voltage
docs.text: The battery supplies 1.5 Joules of energy for every Coulomb of charge.

docs.title: Energy
docs.text: If we accelerate an electron form rest through a potential difference of 1 Volt, then it would gain 1 electron volt () of kinetic energy. Energy is usually expressed in Joules:

docs.title: Energy Conversion
docs.text:
Just like in a gravitational field, in an electric field, potential energy () can be converted into kinetic energy (). For example, if we bring a positive charge close to a fixed, positive point charge, the repulsive force on it gets bigger and bigger. We have to do work on the charge to move it closer, which increases its . When we release the charge, the stored electric potential energy () is converted into !

The total mechanical energy of a system must be conserved.

If the work done by non-conservative forces is zero, then .

docs.title: Types of Kinetic Energy
docs.text:
• : linear or translational
  • : rotational
  • : gravitational
  • : elastic
  • : electrostatic

docs.title: Electric Potential of a Point Charge
docs.text:
An electric potential exists around charges. What is the form of the potential for a point charge?

If we place a positive test charge near a positive fixed point charge, the electric field lines created by the point charge does work on the test charge and moves it to the right.

The work done on the field when moving the charge is . Electric potential due to a point charge: .

docs.title: Equipotential Surfaces
docs.text:
The electric potential of a point charge is . This means the potential is the same in every direction around the point charge at a distance away. This forms a spherical shell of radius around the charge.

Thus, the electric potential is the same everywhere on this spherical surface () - called an equipotential surface.

The electric field lines are perpendicular to the equipotential surface.

In another equipotential surface which is further away but still around the point charge (), there is a lower potential than there is in . Thus, electric field lines point in the direction of decreasing potential (high --> low potential). Therefore, positive charges move in the same direction as the electric field points, and negative charges move in the opposite direction of the field.

docs.title: Work Done on Equipotential Surfaces
docs.text:
The net electric force does no work as a charge moves on a equipotential surface. Why? . But if we're on an equipotential surface, then , and .

(In other words, in order for the charge to feel a force along an equipotential surface, there must be a component of the field along the surface, but is perpendicular to the surface everywhere.)

docs.title: Parallel Plate Capacitor
docs.text:
The positive plate is at a potential of and the negative plate is at . The equipotential surfaces between the planes look like a parallel set of plates.

Let the plates be separated by a distance . The electric field is then (units: ). This is called the electric field gradient.

docs.title: Capacitor
docs.text:
We have two oppositely charged conducting plate separated by some small distance. We charge the plates by connecting them to a battery. The higher the voltage on our battery, the more charge we can put on each plate. Thus, .

. is the capacitance.

*A farad is a very large capacitance. We often use microfarads (: ) and picofarads (: ).

The larger the capacitance, the more charge it will hold!

docs.title: Dielectrics
docs.text:
We can fill the space between the plates with some insulating material, say air, oil, paper, rubber, plastic, etc. This material is called a dielectric.

Since the dielectric is an insulator, the charges in it aren't free to move, but they can separate slightly within each atom. Each one of these atoms now produces a small internal electric fields which points in the opposite direction to the field between the plates.

Thus, the net electric field between the plates is reduced by the dielectric.

The reduction of the field: .

  • field without the dielectric
  • field with the dielectric
  • dielectric constant (must be greater than 1, unitless)
material vacuum 1 air 1.00054 water 80.4

The larger is, the more it reduces the field between the plates.

Let's say the plates have a surface area and are separated by a distance . , but , so .

docs.title: Capacitors store charge. What about energy?
docs.text:
• units:

This expression holds true for any electric fields, not just for capacitors.

docs.title: Electromotive Force
docs.text:
Electrical energy is transferred from a power source, such as a battery, to a device, such as a light bulb. Inside a battery, a chemical reaction separates positive and negative charges, creating a potential difference. This potential difference is equivalent to the battery's voltage, or (electromotive force, which is a potential, not a force).

Because of the on the battery, an electric field is produced within and parallel to the wires. This creates a force on the charges in the wire and moves them around the circuit. This flow of charge in a conductor is called the electrical current ().

Electrical current:

  • units -

If the current only moves in one direction, like with batteries, it's called direct current (). If the current moves in both directions, like in a house, it's called alternating current ().

Electric current is due to the flow of moving electrons, but we will use the positive conventional current in circuit diagrams. shows the direction of "positive" charge flow from high to low potential.

docs.title: Ohm's Law
docs.text:
The battery pushing the current acts like a water pump pushing the water through a pipe. The voltage of the battery is like pump pressure - the larger the voltage, the greater the current. .

Ohm's Law:

  • electrical resistance
  • units -

Resistor: component of an electrical circuit that offers resistance to the flow of electric current.

docs.title: Resistivity
docs.text:
The electrical resistance of a conductor depends on its shape. Longer wires have more resistance; fatter wires have less. Thus, .

Resistivity:

  • electrical resistivity (proportionality constant)
  • units -

Resistivity is an intrinsic property of materials, like density. Every piece of copper has the same resistivity, but the resistance of any one piece depends on its size and shape.

docs.title: Temperature Dependence of Resistivity
docs.text:
The resistance of most materials changes with temperature. For good conductors (metals), the resistance decreases with decreasing temperature. For poor conductors (insulators), the resistance increases with decreasing temperature.

Resistance:

  • resistance at temperature
  • resistance at temperature
  • temperature coefficient of resistivity ( for metals // for insulators)

docs.title: Electrical Power
docs.text: Power: .

docs.title: Electrical Energy
docs.text: Work and energy have the same units (Joules). So, . Electrical companies measure your monthly energy in this way, using kilowatt*hours ().

docs.title: Power Transmission
docs.text:
Electrical power is transmitted at high voltages instead of high currents, because thermal losses (losing electrical power by converting it to heat) is proportional to for a given voltage and proportional to .

//

docs.title: Alternating Current
docs.text:
Circuits where the current periodically switches direction use alternating current (AC). The most common type of AC current is one that varies sinusoidally with time.

Voltage in an AC circuit as a function of time: .

  • peak (max) voltage
  • frequency (Hz)

Since the voltage in an AC circuit oscillates in time, so does the current. , where .

docs.title: Power in an AC Circuit
docs.text:
Just like in a DC circuit, the power is , but since both the current and the voltage fluctuate in time, so does the power. .

Since the power fluctuates in time, we often just reference the average power in the circuit. . We can rewrite the equation for this as: Unless otherwise stated, the voltage in AC circuits will represent the rms voltage, and the power will represent the average power.

docs.title: Series Circuits
docs.text:
A series circuit is when the same current runs through two components connected in series. The voltage drop (potential difference) across resistors is different.

Net resistance (equivalent resistance):

docs.title: Parallel Circuits
docs.text:
A parallel circuit is when the current is split across different wires. For this type of circuit, the voltage drop (potential difference) is the same.

Net resistance (equivalent resistance):

docs.title: Series and Parallel Circuits
docs.text: If resistors are hooked up both in series and in parallel in the same circuit, you must combine resistors to find .

docs.title: Internal Resistance
docs.text:
Batteries and generators have some resistance as well. This is called internal resistance (). In batteries it's due to the chemicals, and in generators it's due to the wire resistance. The voltage across the battery (terminal voltage, ) is less than the full voltage , since some is lost across .

Terminal voltage:

docs.title: Kirchhoff's Rules
docs.text: In many circuits, applying the series or parallel methods is not sufficient to analyze them. There are two other rules we can use called Kirchhoff's Rules.

docs.title: 1. Junction Rule
docs.text: Current into a junction has to equal current out, based on conservation of charge.

docs.title: 2. Loop Rule
docs.text: Around any closed circuit loop, the sum of the potential difference has to equal the sum of the potential rises. (This is based on conservation of energy.)

docs.title: Measuring Voltages and Currents
docs.text: Currents and voltages can be measured with devices called ammeters and voltmeters, respectively. Both these devices rely on the DC galvanometer.

docs.title: Ammeter Resistance
docs.text:
(A circuit must be "broken" to use an ammeter - it needs to be inserted inside the circuit.)

Inside an ammeter, a small resistor () is wired in parallel with the galvanometer. Almost all of the current passes through the shunt resistor, so the ammeter has very little effect on the circuit, i.e. nearly zero resistance.

docs.title: Voltmeter
docs.text: Voltmeter: measures the potential difference between two points in the circuit, i.e. across a resistor. It doesn't have to be inserted in the circuit. A voltmeter has a high resistance, so very little current actually flows through the voltmeter.

docs.text: Capacitors connected in parallel: Capacitors connected in series:

docs.title: RC Circuits
docs.text:
RC Circuit: a resistor is in series with a capacitor. When the switch closes, charge builds up gradually on the capacitor plates as the current flows and approaches an equilibrium value (). If the capacitor is initially uncharged at , then the charge on the capacitor at some later time is .

If , then . We can get the voltage across the capacitor at any time by dividing by the charge by the capacitance, since : .

docs.title: RC
docs.text:
• units -

has the units of time, and the product is known as the time constant of the circuit.

Charging equation:

docs.title: Discharging a Capacitor in an RC Circuit
docs.text:
A capacitor is charged all the way to and then discharged through the resistor. When we close the switch, a current flows through the resistor and charge dissipates from the capacitor plates.

Discharging equation: . At , .
